text.data-narration: Introduction to Self-Study Component (SSC): Case Studies of Air Pollution Episodes

g.text: Self-Study Component (SSC): Case Studies of Air Pollution Episodes

text.data-narration: Air pollution is the release of pollutants into the air that can harm human health, the environment, and the economy.
Common air pollutants include particulate matter (PM), nitrogen oxides (NOx), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).
Primary air pollution is emitted directly from sources such as fossil fuel combustion, industrial processes, and vehicle emissions.
Secondary air pollution is formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions between primary pollutants and other atmospheric compounds.
The 1982 London Smog was caused by a combination of temperature inversion, fog, and high levels of sulfur dioxide emissions from coal-fired power stations and industrial processes.
The smog caused 12,000 deaths over a period of five days and led to the implementation of stricter air quality regulations and the closure of high-polluting industries in London.
The 2001 Indian Heat Wave was caused by a prolonged heat wave that was exacerbated by high levels of particulate matter (PM) pollution.
The heat wave caused an estimated 20,000 to 60,000 deaths over a period of several weeks and led to economic losses estimated at ₹1,000 crore (approximately $140 million USD).
This section provides an overview of the Self-Study Component (SSC): Case Studies of Air Pollution Episodes.
